Dataset 1: /content/Final_Wales_Data.csv¶

In [14]:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder, StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor, GradientBoostingRegressor
from sklearn.svm import SVR
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score, mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_percentage_error
import numpy as np

# Load the DataFrame from the CSV file
final_combined_df = pd.read_csv('/content/Final_Wales_Data.csv')
final_combined_df.head(10)
final_combined_df2= final_combined_df

In [16]:
# List of columns that should be numeric
numeric_columns = ['Number of Projects ', 'Electrical Capacity (MWe) ',
                   'Heat Capacity (MWth) ', 'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ',
                   'Heat Generation (MWhth) ']

# Convert the columns to numeric, forcing errors to NaN (useful if there are any non-numeric values)
for col in numeric_columns:
    final_combined_df[col] = pd.to_numeric(final_combined_df[col].str.replace(',', '').str.strip(), errors='coerce')

# Check the data types after conversion
print(final_combined_df.dtypes)

Wales Regions                     object
Counties                          object
Number of Projects               float64
Electrical Capacity (MWe)        float64
Heat Capacity (MWth)             float64
Electrical Generation (MWhe)     float64
Heat Generation (MWhth)          float64
Technology                        object
Year                               int64
dtype: object

Check for Missing Values in Dataset¶

Missing values Random in diffeernt rows

In [18]:
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Check for missing values
missing_values = final_combined_df.isnull().sum()
print("Missing values in each column:")
print(missing_values)

# Plot heatmap to visualize missing values
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 3))
sns.heatmap(final_combined_df.isnull(), cbar=False, cmap='viridis')
plt.title("Heatmap of Missing Values")
plt.show()

Filling Missing Values¶

If check the distribution of dataset the model is higher than mean or in another words there is saturation or peak recorded at 0, so, fill the missing values with 0 is best choice.

In [19]:
final_combined_df2 = final_combined_df
final_combined_df.fillna(0, inplace=True)
final_combined_df2.fillna(0, inplace=True)

Check distribution of Each variables¶

Plotting Default distribution of each variable without any transformation or scaling in order to check overall distribution of data.

In [21]:
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

numeric_columns = final_combined_df.select_dtypes(include=['float64']).columns

# Set up the matplotlib figure
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))

# Create a histogram for each numeric column
for i, column in enumerate(numeric_columns):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i + 1)  # Adjust subplot dimensions as needed
    sns.histplot(final_combined_df[column], bins=20, kde=True)
    plt.title(f'Distribution of {column}')
    plt.xlabel(column)
    plt.ylabel('Frequency')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

numeric_columns = final_combined_df.select_dtypes(include=['float64']).columns

# Set up the matplotlib figure
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))

# Create a box plot for each numeric column
for i, column in enumerate(numeric_columns):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i + 1)  # Adjust subplot dimensions as needed
    sns.boxplot(x=final_combined_df[column])
    plt.title(f'Box Plot of {column}')
    plt.xlabel(column)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Discussion:¶

  1. From above charts this can be seen that the dataset is heavily Right Skewed, the maximum peak in dataset is recorded around 0 thats why there are many more outliers observed in data.

  2. In order to deal with such charts there is need of transformation that can shift the data from right skewed to normal distribution.

Transformation¶

One major variant of the Box-Cox transformation, which is capable of dealing with positive- as well as negative-valued data, is the Yeo-Johnson transformation. Both of them are like Normalising, the idea of Yeo-Johnson transformation is to make the variance more consistent and bring the data closer to symmetry and resembling a normal distribution.¶

https://medium.com/@vinodkumargr/09-data-transformations-in-ml-different-transformations-in-machine-learning-log-transformer-d794d61d143d

In [22]:
from sklearn.preprocessing import PowerTransformer

# Apply Yeo-Johnson transformation
transformer = PowerTransformer(method='yeo-johnson', standardize=True)
final_combined_df[numeric_columns] = transformer.fit_transform(final_combined_df[numeric_columns])

# Set up the matplotlib figure
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))

# Create a histogram for each numeric column
for i, column in enumerate(numeric_columns):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i + 1)  # Adjust subplot dimensions as needed
    sns.histplot(final_combined_df[column], bins=20, kde=True)
    plt.title(f'Distribution of {column}')
    plt.xlabel(column)
    plt.ylabel('Frequency')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

numeric_columns = final_combined_df.select_dtypes(include=['float64']).columns

# Set up the matplotlib figure
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))

# Create a box plot for each numeric column
for i, column in enumerate(numeric_columns):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i + 1)  # Adjust subplot dimensions as needed
    sns.boxplot(y=final_combined_df[column])
    plt.title(f'Box Plot of {column}')
    plt.xlabel(column)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Discussion:¶

From abobve chart this can be seen that none of the parameters givenb have have not any outliers after using power transformation that shift the data from right skewed to normal distribution. Other than this still their are some outliers in box plot Heat Capacity that need to be considered. So, interquartile range method is best to remove that outlier.

Outliers¶

The aim of the present code is to identify and delete outliers as per the third column ‘Heat Capacity (MWth)’ by applying the IQR method. In the case of a small set of data, the IQR is a simple to get as it is the raw difference between the 25th percentile (Q1) and the 85th percentile (Q3). This means one can determine the measure of dispersion between the point that is 25% below the data and the point that is 85% below the data. The thought process is to concentrate on the middle portion of the data while excluding the lower and upper ranges. If this range is determined, then one can then easily recognize the values which are extremely high and might be termed as outliers. When targeting for the upper bound, it is possible to include potential outliers with values in a higher range than Q3 + 1.5IQR. The remaining outliers are then defined for any number greater than this upper limit. When identified, cleaned the dataset by eradicating these outliers in a bid to get a clearer insight into the other data values. One can address the issue of outliers using this approach, as the greater range of normal data is also incorporated.

In [23]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Specify the column for outlier detection
column_name = 'Heat Capacity (MWth) '

# Calculate Q1, Q3, and IQR
Q1 = final_combined_df[column_name].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = final_combined_df[column_name].quantile(0.85)
IQR = Q3 - Q1

# Calculate upper bound for outliers
upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

# Identify outliers
outliers = final_combined_df[final_combined_df[column_name] > upper_bound]

print("Upper bound for outliers:", upper_bound)
print("Number of outliers detected:", outliers.shape[0])

# Remove outliers from the DataFrame
final_combined_df_cleaned = final_combined_df[final_combined_df[column_name] <= upper_bound]

# Plot distribution again after detecting outliers
plt.figure(figsize=(9, 4))

# Histogram for 'Heat Capacity (MWth)'
plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
sns.histplot(final_combined_df_cleaned[column_name], bins=20, kde=True)
plt.title(f'Distribution of {column_name} (Cleaned)')
plt.xlabel(column_name)
plt.ylabel('Frequency')

# Box plot for 'Heat Capacity (MWth)'
plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
sns.boxplot(y=final_combined_df_cleaned[column_name])
plt.title(f'Box Plot of {column_name} (Cleaned)')
plt.ylabel(column_name)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Upper bound for outliers: 5.003868303963365
Number of outliers detected: 0

Technologies vs. Electrical Capacity¶

In [24]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Unique technologies
unique_technologies = ['Anaerobic Digestion', 'Biomass', 'Energy from Waste',
                       'Biomass Electricity and Combined Heat and Power', 'Heat Pump',
                       'Hydropower', 'Landfill Gas', 'Nuclear', 'Offshore Wind',
                       'Onshore Wind', 'Sewage Gas', 'Solar PV', 'Solar Thermal']

# Loop through each technology and create plots
for technology in unique_technologies:
    # Filter the DataFrame for the current technology
    tech_data = final_combined_df[final_combined_df['Technology'] == technology]

    # Drop NaN values from 'Electrical Capacity (MWe)'
    tech_data = tech_data.dropna(subset=['Electrical Capacity (MWe) '])

    # Check if there's enough data for the technology
    if tech_data.shape[0] < 2:  # Requires at least 2 data points to create a box plot
        print(f"Not enough data available for {technology}. Skipping plot.")
        continue  # Skip to the next technology

    # Box Plot
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)  # Create a subplot for box plot
    sns.boxplot(x='Technology', y='Electrical Capacity (MWe) ', data=tech_data)
    plt.title(f'Box Plot {technology}')
    plt.xlabel('Technology')
    plt.ylabel('Electrical Capacity (MWe)')
    plt.xticks()

    # Distribution Plot
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)  # Create a subplot for distribution
    sns.histplot(tech_data['Electrical Capacity (MWe) '], kde=True, bins=20)
    plt.title(f'Distribution {technology}')
    plt.xlabel('Electrical Capacity (MWe)')
    plt.ylabel('Frequency')

    plt.tight_layout()  # Adjust layout to prevent overlap
    plt.show()

Discussion: ¶

The box plots as well as the distribution graph for Offshore Wind Nuclear and Solar PV technologies are provided below to depict variation in electrical capacity.

The Offshore Wind has certain fluctuations and few extreme values lower and higher than the main values The distribution is totally skewed towards the higher value, most of the values falling close to 1.41 MWe.

On the other hand nuclear is again highly consistent and has no over or under achievers data points with the bars very close to 1.42 MWe showing almost no disparities from the mean in electrical capacity.

However, Solar PV has a large spread of data points and most of them are concentrated on the lower side of electrical capacity indicating higher variability. The distribution raises near 1.4 MWe and has a lower limit with a variable slope that indicates many small solar facilities or other imperfection in data.

Overall, from observation Nuclear Distribution data is not proper that is why Nuclear Distribution data is not enough that’s why appearance of the Box plot is not proper it can be reduced using the outlier removal method IQR.

In [26]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# List of unique technologies
unique_technologies = ['Landfill Gas', 'Onshore Wind', 'Solar PV']

# Specify the column for outlier detection
column_name = 'Electrical Capacity (MWe) '

# Loop through each technology, calculate IQR, remove outliers, and plot
for technology in unique_technologies:
    # Filter data for the specific technology
    tech_data = final_combined_df[final_combined_df['Technology'] == technology]

    # Calculate Q1, Q3, and IQR
    Q1 = tech_data[column_name].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = tech_data[column_name].quantile(0.75)  # Using the typical 75th percentile for outliers
    IQR = Q3 - Q1

    # Calculate upper and lower bounds for outliers
    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

    # Remove outliers from the data
    tech_data_cleaned = tech_data[(tech_data[column_name] >= lower_bound) & (tech_data[column_name] <= upper_bound)]

    # Plotting
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))

    # Box Plot
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)  # Create a subplot for the box plot
    sns.boxplot(x='Technology', y=column_name, data=tech_data_cleaned)
    plt.title(f'Box Plot {technology}')
    plt.xlabel('Technology')
    plt.ylabel('Electrical Capacity (MWe)')

    # Distribution Plot
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)  # Create a subplot for the distribution
    sns.histplot(tech_data_cleaned[column_name], kde=True, bins=20)
    plt.title(f'Distribution {technology}')
    plt.xlabel('Electrical Capacity (MWe)')
    plt.ylabel('Frequency')

    plt.tight_layout()  # Adjust layout to prevent overlap
    plt.show()
In [29]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# List of unique technologies
unique_technologies = ['Solar PV']

# Specify the column for outlier detection
column_name = 'Electrical Capacity (MWe) '

# Loop through each technology, calculate IQR, remove outliers, and plot
for technology in unique_technologies:
    # Filter data for the specific technology
    tech_data = final_combined_df[final_combined_df['Technology'] == technology]

    # Calculate Q1, Q3, and IQR
    Q1 = tech_data[column_name].quantile(0.35)
    Q3 = tech_data[column_name].quantile(0.75)  # Using the typical 75th percentile for outliers
    IQR = Q3 - Q1

    # Calculate upper and lower bounds for outliers
    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    #upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

    # Remove outliers from the data
    tech_data_cleaned = tech_data[(tech_data[column_name] >= lower_bound)]

    # Plotting
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))

    # Box Plot
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)  # Create a subplot for the box plot
    sns.boxplot(x='Technology', y=column_name, data=tech_data_cleaned)
    plt.title(f'Box Plot {technology}')
    plt.xlabel('Technology')
    plt.ylabel('Electrical Capacity (MWe)')

    # Distribution Plot
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)  # Create a subplot for the distribution
    sns.histplot(tech_data_cleaned[column_name], kde=True, bins=20)
    plt.title(f'Distribution {technology}')
    plt.xlabel('Electrical Capacity (MWe)')
    plt.ylabel('Frequency')

    plt.tight_layout()  # Adjust layout to prevent overlap
    plt.show()

Comparison Between Technologies and Regions¶

1. Electrical Capacity (MWe)¶

In [49]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Define the unique technologies for comparison
unique_technologies = ['Anaerobic Digestion', 'Biomass Electricity and Combined Heat and Power', 'Hydropower',
                       'Landfill Gas', 'Onshore Wind', 'Sewage Gas']

# Filter the DataFrame to include only the relevant technologies
tech_data = final_combined_df[final_combined_df['Technology'].isin(unique_technologies)]

# Define a color palette with unique colors for each region
regions = tech_data['Wales Regions'].unique()  # Get unique regions from the data
palette = sns.color_palette("husl", len(regions))  # Create a distinct color palette

# Set up the FacetGrid for separate plots
g = sns.FacetGrid(tech_data, col='Technology', col_wrap=3, height=4, sharey=False)
g.map_dataframe(sns.boxplot, x='Wales Regions', y='Electrical Capacity (MWe) ', hue='Wales Regions', palette=palette, legend=False)

# Add titles and rotate x-axis labels
g.set_titles(col_template='{col_name}')
g.set_axis_labels('Region', 'Electrical Capacity (MWe) ')
for ax in g.axes.flatten():
    ax.set_xticks(range(len(ax.get_xticklabels())))  # Set x-ticks based on the number of labels
    ax.set_xticklabels(ax.get_xticklabels(), rotation=45)

# Add legend for the regions manually
handles = [plt.Line2D([0], [0], marker='o', color='w', markerfacecolor=palette[i], markersize=10) for i in range(len(regions))]
g.fig.legend(handles, regions, title='Wales Regions', loc='upper right', bbox_to_anchor=(1.1, 1))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

2. 'Number of Projects '¶

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Define the unique technologies for comparison
unique_technologies = ['Anaerobic Digestion', 'Biomass Electricity and Combined Heat and Power', 'Hydropower',
                       'Landfill Gas', 'Onshore Wind', 'Sewage Gas']

# Filter the DataFrame to include only the relevant technologies
tech_data = final_combined_df[final_combined_df['Technology'].isin(unique_technologies)]

# Define a color palette with unique colors for each region
regions = tech_data['Wales Regions'].unique()  # Get unique regions from the data
palette = sns.color_palette("husl", len(regions))  # Create a distinct color palette

# Set up the FacetGrid for separate plots
g = sns.FacetGrid(tech_data, col='Technology', col_wrap=3, height=4, sharey=False)
g.map_dataframe(sns.boxplot, x='Wales Regions', y='Number of Projects ', hue='Wales Regions', palette=palette, legend=False)

# Add titles and rotate x-axis labels
g.set_titles(col_template='{col_name}')
g.set_axis_labels('Region', 'Number of Projects ')
for ax in g.axes.flatten():
    ax.set_xticks(range(len(ax.get_xticklabels())))  # Set x-ticks based on the number of labels
    ax.set_xticklabels(ax.get_xticklabels(), rotation=45)

# Add legend for the regions manually
handles = [plt.Line2D([0], [0], marker='o', color='w', markerfacecolor=palette[i], markersize=10) for i in range(len(regions))]
g.fig.legend(handles, regions, title='Wales Regions', loc='upper right', bbox_to_anchor=(1.1, 1))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import seaborn as sns

# Define the unique technologies for comparison
unique_technologies = ['Anaerobic Digestion', 'Biomass Electricity and Combined Heat and Power', 'Hydropower',
                       'Landfill Gas', 'Onshore Wind', 'Sewage Gas']

# Filter the DataFrame to include only the relevant technologies

# Define a color palette with unique colors for each region

palette = sns.color_palette("husl", len(regions))  # Create a distinct color palette

# Set up the FacetGrid for separate plots

g.map_dataframe(sns.boxplot, x='Wales Regions', y='Heat Capacity (MWth) ', hue='Wales Regions', palette=palette, legend=False)

g.set_axis_labels('Region', 'Heat Capacity (MWth) ')
for ax in g.axes.flatten():
    ax.set_xticks(range(len(ax.get_xticklabels())))  # Set x-ticks based on the number of labels

# Add legend for the regions manually

g.fig.legend(handles, regions, title='Wales Regions', loc='upper right', bbox_to_anchor=(1.1, 1))

plt.tight_layout()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Define the unique technologies for comparison
unique_technologies = ['Anaerobic Digestion', 'Biomass Electricity and Combined Heat and Power', 'Hydropower',
                       'Landfill Gas', 'Onshore Wind', 'Sewage Gas']

# Filter the DataFrame to include only the relevant technologies
tech_data = final_combined_df[final_combined_df['Technology'].isin(unique_technologies)]

# Define a color palette with unique colors for each region
regions = tech_data['Wales Regions'].unique()  # Get unique regions from the data
palette = sns.color_palette("husl", len(regions))  # Create a distinct color palette

# Set up the FacetGrid for separate plots
g = sns.FacetGrid(tech_data, col='Technology', col_wrap=3, height=4, sharey=False)
g.map_dataframe(sns.boxplot, x='Wales Regions', y='Heat Generation (MWhth) ', hue='Wales Regions', palette=palette, legend=False)

# Add titles and rotate x-axis labels
g.set_titles(col_template='{col_name}')
g.set_axis_labels('Region', 'Heat Generation (MWhth) ')
for ax in g.axes.flatten():
    ax.set_xticks(range(len(ax.get_xticklabels())))  # Set x-ticks based on the number of labels
    ax.set_xticklabels(ax.get_xticklabels(), rotation=45)

# Add legend for the regions manually
handles = [plt.Line2D([0], [0], marker='o', color='w', markerfacecolor=palette[i], markersize=10) for i in range(len(regions))]
g.fig.legend(handles, regions, title='Wales Regions', loc='upper right', bbox_to_anchor=(1.1, 1))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Correlation Analysis¶

In [78]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

final_combined_df = final_combined_df2

# List of float variables
float_vars = ['Number of Projects ', 'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ', 'Heat Capacity (MWth) ', 'Heat Generation (MWhth) ', 'Electrical Capacity (MWe) ']

# Calculate the correlation matrix
correlation_matrix = final_combined_df[float_vars].corr()

# Set up the matplotlib figure
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))

# Create a heatmap with annotations
sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap='coolwarm', square=True, cbar_kws={"shrink": .8})

# Set titles and labels
plt.title('Correlation Matrix of Float Variables')
plt.xlabel('Variables')
plt.ylabel('Variables')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [77]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

final_combined_df = final_combined_df2

# List of counties
Counties_List = ['Isle of Anglesey ', 'Gwynedd ', 'Conwy ', 'Denbighshire ',
                 'Flintshire ', 'Wrexham ', 'Powys ', 'Ceredigion ',
                 'Pembrokeshire ', 'Carmarthenshire ', 'Swansea ',
                 'Neath Port Talbot ', 'Bridgend ', 'Vale of Glamorgan ',
                 'Cardiff ', 'Rhondda Cynon Taf ', 'Merthyr Tydfil ', 'Caerphilly ',
                 'Blaenau Gwent ', 'Monmouthshire ', 'Newport ']

# List of variables to compare with 'Electrical Capacity (MWe)'
variables = ['Counties', 'Number of Projects ', 'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ', 'Heat Capacity (MWth) ', 'Heat Generation (MWhth) ']

# Loop through variables and plot
for var in variables:
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 3))

    if final_combined_df[var].dtype in ['float64']:  # Continuous variables
        sns.scatterplot(x=final_combined_df[var], y=final_combined_df['Electrical Capacity (MWe) '])
        plt.title(f'{var} vs Electrical Capacity (MWe)')
        plt.xlabel(var)
        plt.ylabel('Electrical Capacity (MWe)')

    else:  # Categorical variables
        # Check if the variable is 'Counties' and only include categories from Counties_List
        if var == 'Counties':
            filtered_data = final_combined_df[final_combined_df[var].isin(Counties_List)]
            plt.figure(figsize=(10, 3))
            sns.boxplot(x=filtered_data[var], y=filtered_data['Electrical Capacity (MWe) '])
            plt.title(f'{var} vs Electrical Capacity (MWe)')
            plt.xlabel(var)
            plt.ylabel('Electrical Capacity (MWe)')
            plt.xticks(rotation=90)  # Rotate category labels

        else:  # Other categorical variables not in Counties_List
            plt.figure(figsize=(10, 3))
            sns.boxplot(x=final_combined_df[var], y=final_combined_df['Electrical Capacity (MWe) '])
            plt.title(f'{var} vs Electrical Capacity (MWe)')
            plt.xlabel(var)
            plt.ylabel('Electrical Capacity (MWe)')
            plt.xticks(rotation=90)  # Rotate category labels

    plt.show()

<Figure size 1000x300 with 0 Axes>

Trend Analysis and forecasting for next 5 years using ARIMA¶

In [82]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

final_combined_df = final_combined_df2.copy()  # Create a copy of the dataset

# Aggregate data by year for trend analysis
trend_data = final_combined_df.groupby('Year').agg({
    'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ': 'sum',
    'Electrical Capacity (MWe) ': 'sum',
    'Heat Generation (MWhth) ': 'sum',
    'Heat Capacity (MWth) ': 'sum',
    'Number of Projects ': 'count'  # Count of projects per year
}).reset_index()

# Set the figure size
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))

# Plot trends for Electrical Generation and Capacity
plt.plot(trend_data['Year'], trend_data['Electrical Generation (MWhe) '], marker='o', label='Electrical Generation (MWhe)')
plt.plot(trend_data['Year'], trend_data['Electrical Capacity (MWe) '], marker='o', label='Electrical Capacity (MWe)')

# Add titles and labels
plt.title('Trend Analysis of Electrical Generation and Capacity Over Years')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Sum Values')
plt.xticks(trend_data['Year'])  # Rotate x-axis labels for better visibility
plt.legend()
plt.grid()

# Show the plot
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

In [88]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pmdarima import auto_arima
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA

# Create a copy of the dataset
final_combined_df = final_combined_df2.copy()

# Aggregate data by year
trend_data = final_combined_df.groupby('Year').agg({
    'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ': 'sum',
    'Electrical Capacity (MWe) ': 'sum'
}).reset_index()

# Forecasting function
def forecast_series(data, variable, steps=5):
    model = auto_arima(data, seasonal=False, trace=True, error_action='ignore', suppress_warnings=True)
    model_fit = model.fit(data)

    # Forecast
    future_forecast = model_fit.predict(n_periods=steps)
    return future_forecast

# Define the number of future years to predict
future_years = 5

# Forecast for Electrical Generation
generation_forecast = forecast_series(trend_data['Electrical Generation (MWhe) '], 'Electrical Generation', steps=future_years)

# Forecast for Electrical Capacity
capacity_forecast = forecast_series(trend_data['Electrical Capacity (MWe) '], 'Electrical Capacity', steps=future_years)

# Create a new DataFrame for future predictions
future_years_index = np.arange(trend_data['Year'].max() + 1, trend_data['Year'].max() + 1 + future_years)
future_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Year': future_years_index,
    'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ': generation_forecast,
    'Electrical Capacity (MWe) ': capacity_forecast
})

# Combine historical and forecasted data for plotting
combined_df = pd.concat([trend_data, future_df], ignore_index=True)

# Plot the results
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
plt.plot(combined_df['Year'], combined_df['Electrical Generation (MWhe) '], marker='o', label='Electrical Generation (MWhe)')
plt.plot(combined_df['Year'], combined_df['Electrical Capacity (MWe) '], marker='o', label='Electrical Capacity (MWe)')
plt.axvline(x=trend_data['Year'].max(), color='gray', linestyle='--', label='Forecast Start')
plt.title('Forecasting Electrical Generation and Capacity')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Values (MW)')
plt.xticks(combined_df['Year'])
plt.legend()
plt.grid()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Electrical Generation vs. All Technologies for Next 5 Years¶

In [105]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pmdarima import auto_arima

# Create a copy of the dataset
final_combined_df = final_combined_df2.copy()

# Define the number of future years to predict
future_years = 5

# Set the specific technologies to focus on
selected_technologies = [
    'Offshore Wind',
    'Onshore Wind',
    'Anaerobic Digestion',
    'Solar PV',
    'Biomass Electricity and Combined Heat and Power']

# Set up a plot for all selected technologies
plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5))

# Loop through each selected technology
for tech in selected_technologies:
    # Filter data for the specific technology
    tech_data = final_combined_df[final_combined_df['Technology'] == tech]

    # Aggregate data by year for the technology
    trend_data = tech_data.groupby('Year').agg({
        'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ': 'sum',
    }).reset_index()

    # Forecasting function
    def forecast_series(data, steps=5):
        model = auto_arima(data, seasonal=False, trace=False, error_action='ignore', suppress_warnings=True)
        model_fit = model.fit(data)
        future_forecast = model_fit.predict(n_periods=steps)
        return future_forecast

    # Forecast for Electrical Generation
    generation_forecast = forecast_series(trend_data['Electrical Generation (MWhe) '], steps=future_years)

    # Create a new DataFrame for future predictions
    future_years_index = np.arange(trend_data['Year'].max() + 1, trend_data['Year'].max() + 1 + future_years)
    future_df = pd.DataFrame({
        'Year': future_years_index,
        'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ': generation_forecast,
        'Technology': tech
    })

    # Combine historical and forecasted data for plotting
    combined_df = pd.concat([trend_data.assign(Technology=tech), future_df], ignore_index=True)

    # Plot the results for this technology
    plt.plot(combined_df['Year'], combined_df['Electrical Generation (MWhe) '], marker='o', label=f'{tech} - Generation (MWhe)')

# Final plot settings
plt.axvline(x=trend_data['Year'].max(), color='gray', linestyle='--', label='Forecast Start')
plt.title('Forecasting Electrical Generation by Selected Technologies')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Electrical Generation (MW)')
plt.xticks(combined_df['Year'])
plt.legend()
plt.grid()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Electrical Generation and ACF and PACF Plots¶

  1. 'Offshore Wind',
  2. 'Onshore Wind',
  3. 'Solar PV'
In [121]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pmdarima import auto_arima
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf

# Create a copy of the dataset
final_combined_df = final_combined_df2.copy()

# Define the number of future years to predict
future_years = 5

# Set the specific technologies to focus on
selected_technologies = [
    'Solar PV',
    'Offshore Wind',
    'Onshore Wind']

# Loop through each selected technology
for tech in selected_technologies:
    # Filter data for the specific technology
    tech_data = final_combined_df[final_combined_df['Technology'] == tech]

    # Aggregate data by year for the technology
    trend_data = tech_data.groupby('Year').agg({
        'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ': 'sum',
    }).reset_index()

    # Ensure there are enough data points for ACF/PACF
    n_lags = min(10, len(trend_data) - 1, len(trend_data) // 2)  # At most half the sample size

    # Forecasting function
    def forecast_series(data, steps=5):
        model = auto_arima(data, seasonal=False, trace=False, error_action='ignore', suppress_warnings=True)
        model_fit = model.fit(data)
        future_forecast = model_fit.predict(n_periods=steps)
        return future_forecast

    # Forecast for Electrical Generation
    generation_forecast = forecast_series(trend_data['Electrical Generation (MWhe) '], steps=future_years)

    # Create a new DataFrame for future predictions
    future_years_index = np.arange(trend_data['Year'].max() + 1, trend_data['Year'].max() + 1 + future_years)
    future_df = pd.DataFrame({
        'Year': future_years_index,
        'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ': generation_forecast,
        'Technology': tech
    })

    # Combine historical and forecasted data for plotting
    combined_df = pd.concat([trend_data.assign(Technology=tech), future_df], ignore_index=True)

    # Plot the results for this technology
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 3))
    plt.plot(combined_df['Year'], combined_df['Electrical Generation (MWhe) '], marker='o', label=f'{tech} - Generation (MWhe)')
    plt.title(f'Forecasting Electrical Generation by {tech}')
    # Set up a plot for all selected technologies

    # ACF and PACF plots
    plt.figure(figsize=(7, 3))

    # ACF plot
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
    plot_acf(trend_data['Electrical Generation (MWhe) '], lags=n_lags, ax=plt.gca())
    plt.title(f'ACF {tech}')

    # PACF plot
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
    plot_pacf(trend_data['Electrical Generation (MWhe) '], lags=n_lags, ax=plt.gca())
    plt.title(f'PACF {tech}')

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Dataset 2: /content/global_renewable_energy_production.csv¶

In [159]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor, GradientBoostingRegressor
from sklearn.svm import SVR
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score, mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_percentage_error

# Load data
file_path = '/content/global_renewable_energy_production.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(file_path)

# Print missing values
print("\nMissing Values:")
print(df.isnull().sum())

# Print duplicate values
print("\nDuplicate Rows:")
print(df.duplicated().sum())

# Select numeric columns
numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['float64']).columns

# Set up the subplot grid (6 rows, 2 columns)
n_rows = 6
n_cols = 2
fig, axes = plt.subplots(n_rows, n_cols, figsize=(10, 20))
axes = axes.flatten()  # Flatten the 2D array of axes

# Create box plots and distribution plots side by side
for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols):
    if i < n_rows:  # Limit to the number of subplots
        # Box plot
        sns.boxplot(data=df, y=col, ax=axes[i * 2])
        axes[i * 2].set_title(f'Box Plot of {col}')
        axes[i * 2].set_ylabel(col)
        axes[i * 2].grid()

        # Distribution plot
        sns.histplot(df[col], kde=True, ax=axes[i * 2 + 1], bins=20)
        axes[i * 2 + 1].set_title(f'Distribution of {col}')
        axes[i * 2 + 1].set_ylabel('Frequency')
        axes[i * 2 + 1].grid()

# Hide any unused subplots
for j in range(i + 1, n_rows):
    fig.delaxes(axes[j * 2])
    fig.delaxes(axes[j * 2 + 1])

plt.tight_layout()
plt.subplots_adjust(hspace=0.5, wspace=0.2)  # Adjust horizontal and vertical space
plt.show()

Year                    0
Country                 0
SolarEnergy             0
WindEnergy              0
HydroEnergy             0
OtherRenewableEnergy    0
TotalRenewableEnergy    0
dtype: int64

Duplicate Rows:
0

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

columns = ['Year', 'SolarEnergy', 'WindEnergy', 'HydroEnergy','OtherRenewableEnergy', 'TotalRenewableEnergy']
for col in columns:
  # Set up the plot
  plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))

  # Create a box plot
  sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Country', y=col, palette='viridis')
  # Customize the plot
  plt.title(f'Box Plot of {col} by Country')
  plt.xlabel('Country')
  plt.ylabel(f'{col}')
  plt.xticks()
  plt.grid(axis='y')
  plt.tight_layout()

  # Show the plot
  plt.show()

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.boxplot(data=df, x='Country', y=col, palette='viridis')

Forecasting all energy sources for next 3 years using ARIMA¶

In [204]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pmdarima import auto_arima

# Aggregate data by year
trend_data = df.groupby('Year').agg({
    'SolarEnergy': 'sum',
    'WindEnergy': 'sum',
    'HydroEnergy': 'sum',
    'OtherRenewableEnergy': 'sum',
    'TotalRenewableEnergy': 'sum'
}).reset_index()

# Forecasting function
def forecast_series(data, steps=5):
    model = auto_arima(data, seasonal=False, trace=True, error_action='ignore', suppress_warnings=True)
    model_fit = model.fit(data)

    # Forecast
    future_forecast = model_fit.predict(n_periods=steps)
    return future_forecast

# Define the number of future years to predict
future_years = 3

# Create a new DataFrame for future predictions
future_years_index = np.arange(trend_data['Year'].max() + 1, trend_data['Year'].max() + 1 + future_years)

# Store forecasted values
forecast_results = {
    'Year': future_years_index,
    'SolarEnergy': forecast_series(trend_data['SolarEnergy'], steps=future_years),
    'WindEnergy': forecast_series(trend_data['WindEnergy'], steps=future_years),
    'HydroEnergy': forecast_series(trend_data['HydroEnergy'], steps=future_years),
    'OtherRenewableEnergy': forecast_series(trend_data['OtherRenewableEnergy'], steps=future_years),
    'TotalRenewableEnergy': forecast_series(trend_data['TotalRenewableEnergy'], steps=future_years)
}

# Create a DataFrame for future predictions
future_df = pd.DataFrame(forecast_results)

# Combine historical and forecasted data for plotting
combined_df = pd.concat([trend_data, future_df], ignore_index=True)

# Plot the results
plt.figure(figsize=(16, 6))

# Plot each energy type
for column in ['SolarEnergy', 'WindEnergy', 'HydroEnergy', 'OtherRenewableEnergy', 'TotalRenewableEnergy']:
    plt.plot(combined_df['Year'], combined_df[column], marker='o', label=column)

plt.axvline(x=trend_data['Year'].max(), color='gray', linestyle='--', label='Forecast Start')
plt.title('Forecasting Renewable Energy Production')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Values')
plt.xticks(combined_df['Year'])
plt.legend()
plt.grid()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Correlation Analysis between two dataseta¶

In [181]:
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Assuming final_combined_df and df are already loaded

# Merge the DataFrames on the Year column
merged_df = pd.merge(final_combined_df, df, on='Year', how='inner')

# Check the shape of the merged DataFrame
print("Merged DataFrame Shape:", merged_df.shape)

# Select numerical columns for correlation
numeric_cols = [
    #'Number of Projects ',
    'Electrical Capacity (MWe) ',
    'Heat Capacity (MWth) ',
    'Electrical Generation (MWhe) ',
    #'Heat Generation (MWhth) ',
    'SolarEnergy',
    'WindEnergy',
    #'HydroEnergy',
    'OtherRenewableEnergy',
    'TotalRenewableEnergy'
]

# Calculate the correlation matrix
correlation_matrix = merged_df[numeric_cols].corr()

# Visualize the correlation matrix
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt=".2f")
plt.title('Correlation Matrix')
plt.show()

Merged DataFrame Shape: (28080, 15)
